title: Rose geranium cold-process soap
subtitle: A floral bar with sweet almond oil, olive oil, and rose geranium essential oil
excerpt: Sweet almond oil at 15% of the oil weight gives this cold-process bar a soft, skin-conditioning lather. Rose geranium essential oil, added at light trace, produces a warm, rosy-herbal scent that holds through the 6-week cure. The bar is uncoloured, a pale cream, finished with dried rose petals pressed into the top for decoration.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128, sweet almond oil 0.136. 5% superfat; 33% lye discount. Rose geranium (Pelargonium graveolens) EO in cold-process soap: scent retention is moderate; tested usage rate 2.4% of oil weight. Sweet almond oil SAP value reference: AOCS lipid library. Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997).

recipe.batchNotes: Recalculate lye for any change to oil weights using a lye calculator. Note that sweet almond oil has a SAP value of 0.136, slightly higher than olive oil.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: 48 hours in the mould; 4 to 6 weeks on a curing rack before use.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool dark place on an open rack. Discard if bars develop orange oily spots (DOS) or smell sharp and rancid. Note: sweet almond oil has a shorter shelf life than olive oil; bars containing sweet almond oil are more susceptible to DOS if stored in warm or damp conditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The portion of oil left unsaponified in the finished bar, expressed as a percentage of the total oil weight. A 5% superfat leaves free conditioning oil in the bar.

The moment in cold-process soap making when the batter thickens to a warm-custard consistency and holds a surface drizzle briefly. The point at which essential oils and additives are stirred in before pouring.

content.text: Rose geranium essential oil sits at 2.4% of the oil weight in this bar, which is within the recommended usage for a rinse-off product and gives a warm, rosy-herbal scent that softens but holds through the 6-week cure. Sweet almond oil at 15% contributes linoleic acid and a lathering quality that is less hard-scrubbing than a pure coconut oil bar. The oil base is 52% olive for conditioning, 28% coconut for lather and hardness, 15% sweet almond for skin feel, and 5% castor for lather stability. At 5%
the bar is mild. This contains tree nuts (sweet almond oil); patch test before use if nut sensitivity is a concern.

items.prepNote: 28% of oil weight; melted if solid
15% of oil weight; contains tree nuts
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat across the blended oil SAP values
2.4% of oil weight; added at light trace

content.text: Put on protective gloves and goggles. Work in a ventilated space. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add the sodium hydroxide in a slow, steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never add water to lye. Leave to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Weigh the olive oil, sweet almond oil, and castor oil into a stainless-steel bowl. Melt the coconut oil and add it to the bowl. Cool the blended oils to 38 to 42°C.
Blend to trace and add the essential oil
Pour the lye solution into the oils in a steady stream. Blend in short 5-second bursts until the batter reaches light
. Stir in the rose geranium essential oil and blend for a further 3 seconds. Pour into the silicone loaf mould, tap once, and wrap in a towel. Leave for 48 hours.
Unmould after 48 hours. Wear gloves. Cut into bars with the wire cutter. Cure on a rack for 4 to 6 weeks, turning the bars every 2 weeks. The scent softens and blends during cure.

Store cured bars on an open rack in a cool dark place. Bars keep two years. Bars containing sweet almond oil are more susceptible to dreaded orange spots (DOS, rancid superfat) than olive-oil-only bars; store in a cool, dry environment. Discard immediately if DOS appears or if the bar smells sharp and paint-like.
